content.problemStatement:
You are given a circular binary string SSS of length NNN.

That is, position 111 is adjacent to positions 222 and NNN, and in general position iii is adjacent to (i−1)(i-1)(i−1) and (i+1)(i+1)(i+1) (with indices taken modulo NNN).

You are also given a starting index KKK.

You must delete all NNN characters from the string, one at a time, following these rules:


Your score starts at 000.
The first deletion must be the character at position KKK.
After deleting a character at some position ppp, the next deletion must be from one of the two characters currently adjacent to ppp in the remaining circular structure.
Let the character deleted on the iii-th move be c∈{0,1}c \in \{0,1\}c∈{0,1} (treated as an integer).

If iii is odd, your score becomes score+c\text{score} + cscore+c.
If iii is even, your score becomes score−c\text{score} - cscore−c.


After every deletion, including the last one, your score must remain strictly positive.
Your task is to determine whether such a deletion sequence exists.

If it exists, output one valid sequence of NNN deleted positions.

If it does not exist, print −1-1−1 instead.


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of two lines of input.

The first line of each test case contains two space-separated integers NNN and KKK — the length of the binary string and starting position from which the first deletion must be made, respectively.
The second line contains the binary string SSS of length NNN.




Output Format
For each test case, determine whether such a deletion sequence exists.



If no valid deletion sequence exists, output the single integer −1-1−1.
If a valid sequence does exist, output NNN space-separated integers denoting the deletion sequence you found.

The sequence should contain each index from 111 to NNN exactly once.

The index represents the position of the deleted element in the original string.
constraints: The sum of NNN over all test cases won't exceed 2⋅1052\cdot10^52⋅105.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test case 111: Since N=2N = 2N=2 and K=1K = 1K=1, the only possible deletion sequence is [1,2][1, 2][1,2].

On the first move, we delete S1=1S_1 = 1S1​=1. Our score increases by 111, and now equals 111.
On the second move, we delete S2=1S_2 = 1S2​=1. Our score decreases by 111, and now equals 000.

Since we failed to keep the score strictly positive, no valid deletion sequence exists.
Test case 222: Again, the only possible deletion sequence is [1,2][1, 2][1,2].
This time the score sequence is [0,−1][0, -1][0,−1], and once again since it doesn't remain strictly positive, no solution exists.
Test case 333: Now, the only possible deletion sequence is [2,1][2, 1][2,1].
The corresponding score sequence is [1,1][1, 1][1,1], and it is strictly positive always so we obtained a valid deletion sequence.
Test case 444: The score sequence corresponding to deletion sequence [4,3,1,2][4, 3, 1, 2][4,3,1,2] is [1,1,2,2][1, 1, 2, 2][1,1,2,2], which is always strictly positive.
